elements.sent-2411-006: Tangles are an abstract notion of `clusters' in graphs that originates in the theory of graph minors developed by Robertson and Seymour‾¥¥cite{GM .}
elements.sent-2411-007: They allow for a unified treatment of various concrete highly cohesive substructures in graphs, such as large clique or grid minors.
elements.sent-2411-008: Tangles describe these clusters in a graph indirectly.
elements.sent-2411-009: Instead of describing what the cluster is composed of, they describe its position, in that they orient the low-order separations of the graph towards it.
elements.sent-2411-010: Intuitively, a concrete cluster orients all low-order separations by majority vote, that is the cluster orients such a separation $¥¥{A,B¥¥}$ towards its side, $A$ or $B$, which contains most of the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-011: Such a side exists; otherwise, the cluster would be separated by few vertices, which contradicts its high cohesion.
elements.sent-2411-012: The orientations of the low-order separations induced by concrete clusters are `consistent', in that they all point to the cluster.
